items.misconception: 2/5 — by adding numerators and denominators separately.
items.why: Students treat fraction addition as elementwise, applying the rule for fraction multiplication (which does multiply numerators and denominators) to addition.

items.misconception: 15, because 15% means 15 units out of any amount.
items.why: Students treat 'X percent of' as if X is the answer directly, not as a proportion to apply to the amount.

items.q: Which average is best to use for the data set [2, 3, 3, 4, 4, 4, 5, 100]?
items.correct: The median (3.5) — it isn't distorted by the outlier 100, unlike the mean.
items.misconception: The mean — it always gives the most accurate average.
items.random_wrong: The mode, because the most common value is always the best representative.
items.why: Students treat 'the mean' as the default for every data set, without considering whether outliers make a different measure of centre more appropriate.

items.q: A force of 10 N acts perpendicular to a rod, 0.5 m from the pivot. Moment is:
items.why: It's 5 Nm. “20 Nm” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: For a body in equilibrium, the sum of moments about any point is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: zero. “positive” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Find the moment of a 20 N force acting perpendicular to a lever, 2 m from the pivot.
items.why: The real figure is 40 Nm. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
